speaker: interviewer
text: Roll one. Roll. I don't get channel two now. What? Oh, yeah I do. This is roll one with Mollie Stauber. Roll one, Mollie Stauber. Roll one with Mollie Stauber. January 19, 1995. The survivor's name is Mollie Stauber. The interviewer is Ellen Baskin. The city, Beverly Hills. Country, United States of America. And conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin by asking you what your name is and where and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Malka Menglovitz. And I was both in March 4, 1926 in Bilky. At the time, it was Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a community was Bilky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bilky was a small community, mostly farming, and this was about all. It had about 300 Jews in the community-- very primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it mostly Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was mostly Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my family-- my father, David Menglovitz, was a butcher. And he was also, during the summer, he was in the produce. He would buy them and ship them all over Czechoslovakia. Life was primitive. My mother, Regina Pascowitz, she came from a neighboring community, a very wealthy family. My grandfather in those years, he was head of a bank. And but he-- her father passed away when she was married. I never-- I never knew my grandfather from my mother's side, only my grandmother. And they had-- she had six sisters and three brothers. They-- all the sisters except one here in the United States perished. And life was primitive, but we didn't know any better, so it was accepted. And my, my father's aim was to leave the community. His brothers were in the-- his brothers and-- two brothers and two sisters were here in the United States already, and his aim was very much to leave. My mother objected to it because she would never leave her family. She was very, very attached to her mother, and she was a widow who lived alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she always-- I remember as a very young child, that she said that she would never leave while her mother is alive, even though she had a sister in the United States. And that's the only sister that survived. Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I had-- I'm the oldest. I had-- we were six children. One, my sister, Rosalyn, she's the only one that was with me all through the Holocaust, and she survived. And the rest, three brothers and a younger sister, perished with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Bilky?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only one main street, and we lived on that main street. It wasn't even called a main street. Everybody knew everybody, so there was no addresses or anything. It was just a name, and everybody knew everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community was all orthodox. There was only one synagogue. And the rabbi was-- knew everybody also, lived very close by. And they had Hebrew schools for their children. And the boys were going from 5 o'clock in the morning till 7:30 to the-- it was called a cheder, the Hebrew school. And then 7:30, they were going to public school. And then after school, they continued the cheder and stayed till about 7 o'clock, the boys, from age three until after the bar mitzvah. This was the norm of the boys. The girls were taken home, private lessons sometimes. Sometimes from the older children would learn. And i was going into a public school, the Czech school, which was an excellent school. We were taught a lot of things that was very, very helpful in life later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Such as music, languages. It was mandatory-- in the fourth grade, it was mandatory to take two additional languages, and read music, and also home economics. We'd call it knitting, crocheting, embroidery, cooking, serving properly. All this was given to us. And it was liberal. The schools were liberal. There was also that was called Russian speaking school-- not Russian, Ukraine or whatever, Slavic also. So the Jewish kids mostly went to Czech schools. And also the Czech officials' children were going to the Czech school. And the other wasn't as good. And this was until 1938. Excuse me. In 1938, they mobilized. That's when Hitler declared war on Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's fine. They mobilized all the men until the age of 42. They were hoping that they're going to fight Hitler. So my father was among them. And that's when the Czech school has closed down because there were no teachers. And my father stayed on until Hitler had occupied Czechoslovakia. Not our part of the country, but in our part of the country, they let the-- they, they forced the Ukraines-- they were more in the Ukraine. And you know, I was a kid, and I don't even know whether it was Ukraine or it was Russian. That they forced the Czech out of our part of the country. The Carpathian was called the Carpathian. And they forced them out. And that's when hell broke out because the Ukraines came and occupied our part of the country for a while. And the first things that they started out on the Jews. They were beating them and robbing them and shooting in the streets and going into the synagogue and pull out Jews and beat them up. And my father was in the service until 1940 or something like that. Then he came home for a little while. And eventually-- I don't know exactly the, the year. But it was in 1941, we were occupied by the Hungarians. They threw out the Ukraines, and the Hungarians came in. So they had again started out on the Jews again all over. It was-- for a few months, it was OK. But then they turned on the Jews. And it was miserable again. We were rationed a little food. And they took away the license from the Jews. Like my father was a butcher. He wasn't allowed to kill-- slaughtering, excuse me, slaughtering the animals. Or having the butcher store, this was forbidden for the Jews. So they would bribe, sometimes, officials that would let him in the non-kosher slaughter house have the schochet, which was called, come in and slaughter, that the Jews should have a little meat. But this was-- so they-- those that they were working hand in hand, they would give them the non-kosher part of the meat because, you know, there is an animal that is kosher part and non-kosher part. So they let them have it for nothing. But then they went and they turned on them. And they called up the authorities and say go confiscate. And they would arrest them and they would torture them to say. So it was hell through the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had it been like between the Jews and the non-Jews in Bilky before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- while Czechoslovakia-- while we were under Czechoslovakia, it was liberal. There was no anti-Semitism. Maybe they were holding it, but they-- it wasn't and open anti-Semitism. But when they had the authority, they took advantage fully.

speaker: interviewer
text: People in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the community, yes. They would-- any little thing that they saw that they knew that wasn't right, they reported the Jew. Those that had license on selling various things, they would say, on the QT, he is selling it. And they would-- they would always find reasons. Like for instance, even later one we were given rations. So they would report, she sold her rations. My mother was mostly alone because my father was again in the service from 1941 or '42, taken in the Hungarian service as a Jew. Not in a uniform, but with civilian clothes, with the yellow bands, with the stars. And so she was basically alone with the children. So she would serve the rations. Like for instance, sugar, we were six children. So we got the sugar she would sell for produce that she could use for the children. And it was very, very miserable-- very miserable. They would give us the rations one week. And the next week they would come and raid the whole house, look whether there was no additional produce or something, or confiscate even what was legally bought on the-- with the rations. They would confiscate it. So there was tremendous poverty and hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing yellow stars at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were wearing yellow stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they given out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe they were given out '40, maybe '42. Can't remember exactly the dates. But I know that my brothers, when they would go early in the morning to the cheder, they would sometimes be attacked by the hoodlums. These were very young children because my oldest brother was only 13, 11, and six or seven-- younger, younger, younger. When they perished, they were only that age. And they would be attacked by them, but in the evening when they would be coming home, they would beat the kids up. And the schools, while we were in the schools, the Jewish boys were kicked, spit at, pulled at their-- most of them, the orthodox wore the peyas-- you know what it is-- pulled them. Take off their hats because they were Jews, and beat on them. Everything was permissible. There was no-- nothing was stopped. A Jew, everything was OK to do. We had neighbors that my parents were very, very close with because they lived there for many years. And my mother helped her because she-- the women especially, one neighbor, she had heart condition. So she was unable to do a lot of things. My mother would help her. And I can never forget her face and her family's face that when we were being deported, how happy they were all and how they were waving and smiling at us. And this is why I could never go back to that part of the country because it remained a very, very, very vivid picture in my-- in my mind. And I never went, never. I did go back up, bit of hope that maybe somebody returned from my family and I'll find somebody. But was just for a very, very short period. And then left it. But that memory always remained with me. As a matter of fact, at one time I wanted my daughter to see where it all began, what it was all about. And then when I started thinking about it, makes me so sick inside that I says, I don't think I-- I don't think I can make it because if you go with hatred and bitterness-- hatred not even so much, but bitterness. You remember their reaction towards us. So just, just couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in 1944-- is? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, the Jews were-- my father was home already at that time from the service. He came back in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you about the things that he had seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father-- my father was a chef sort of in the Jewish units, in the Jewish army. He was the chef. And he did the cooking. So he was-- he was shipped out by the Hungarians to the Ukraine. And there, his unit was burying the deads that-- the Germans were killing the Jews in the Ukraine and destroying the synagogues. The vegetables were wrapped in expensive books that were torn out or from Torahs. And he found a Torah. And he rescued that Torah. He kept it with his kitchen equipment because one day, they just told him, you're free to go without any papers, without anything. And as a Jew with the yellow star and with the yellow band, they could go maybe a half a block and then be killed because they had no papers. Nobody believed them who they are or what they are. They just are a Ukraine Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day they just let him go. His, his officers, his high officers, they even got fed up with the whole system and they-- I don't know whether they have the-- you know, they were so close to the Russian border. Some of them escaped to Russia. And some of them, don't know. But they just let him go. And my father packed up that Torah among his kitchen utensils. And he came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened in 1943 on Yom Kippur, just before. They walked most of the time at night because during the day, they couldn't move. He came home Yom Kippur morning. And that Yom Kippur day, it was all in the paper that they all escaped unit and every one of them is going to be hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the rabbi told him, you stay in. Do your prayers on Yom Kippur. And then after Yom Kippur, you go and report yourself to the authorities that you came back and what happened. And he did. And he was immediately arrested. You were saying that your father turned himself in and was arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was arrested, was shipped back to his unit. But until they, they found out what really happened, he was under arrest. And then they let him go. And he was still chef in Hungary, in his unit. They kept him there, a Jewish unit. And then all his kitchen utensils, with all the things, came back. The Torah came back. And he was able to bring that Torah home, and gave it to synagogue. It was a-- it was a very big thing that a Torah from the Ukraine was rescued.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in your local synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the local synagogue. And then, maybe a year later, it was destroyed with everything else, just like everything else was destroyed. It was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like when you saw these things being destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't. That was after we were-- when we just came back, we, we have seen. A year back when I came back for a few days, and only the talk that what they have done to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you deported from Bilky?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were deported. And they gave us-- they were very nice to us. They let us finish the Passover. And the last day--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, the last day, they have rounded up all the  Jews, whatever you could carry. So in 1944, we had the last supper together as a family, Pesach. And in 1944, we were rounded up, all the Jews, in the synagogue. And what did I say? We were able to carry. That's all you took with them. First thing, they started up any valuable possessions, if you have, surrender it immediately because if you find it on you, you're going to be severely punished. So Bilky wasn't a very rich community, and there wasn't very much. So they surrendered whatever they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you packed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have packed-- my parents packed a little bit of bedding, and clothes for us, and a couple of pots and pans and as far as the little food that they had, which wasn't much. And everybody had a bundle. And that's all. But this is about all. We have given certain things to the neighbors if they wanted to hide it for us. My mother had a lot of-- she came from a wealthy family, as a I told you-- a lot of hand embroidered things, which that was part of the trousseau for her at that time that everything was embroidered. Her linens, her tablecloths, her towels, everything was hand made.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she gave them. They should hide it for it, that maybe she will-- somebody will return. We never, definitely never, never thought-- but my father-- but he didn't talk about it-- saw what happened. And he feared tremendously that this is going to happen to our part of the country also.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, they begged the minister from the church-- because there was illness-- that he should declare that it is a-- because a couple of people had typhus, that he should declare the community as a with a contagious disease. They should be held back. He refused. He was extremely anti-Semitic. Sundays were the most horrible, horrible days because he would preach in the church, and then they would go out on the church and find their victims. The Jews were hidden, locked up in their homes. They were-- they were terrified, because that's when he antagonized them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any extra responsibilities as the eldest child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed a great deal with my grandmother because she was a bit old. She was alone. Yes, my grandmother still had-- she had a big farm. And one time she had a lot of help. But in the later years, she had nothing. So I had to help out with those chores. And my grandmother passed away in 1943. It was a very grateful thing that she didn't have to go through. And we had-- also we had some cattle. So either you went out with your cows because things were extremely rough. So the kids-- either me, my sister, or my brother-- were taking care of the cattle. And this was about the only-- schools, the Jews were kicked out when the Hungarians came in. So you couldn't go to school. Reading, whatever you could put something, your hands on it. This is about the only thing. And no big responsibilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they gathered up your community, where did they take you all to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have taken us all to-- with the train-- to a city. It was called-- also Czechoslovakia. I mean, it was Hungary at that time already-- Berehovo or Beregszasz. In Czech, they called it Berehovo. In Hungarian, they called it Beregszasz. And they brought us there with the train. It was a brick factory. So all it had is a roof because that's where they were drying the bricks. And that's where they dumped us all. From the train, they took us to the brick factory. Everybody had a little space, just, you know, to be able to lay down. And that's where they put out the bedding, took a sheet and sort of tried to tie it to the roof, that the wind shouldn't-- because it was April. It was still very cold in Europe. And that's where we slept. When the rain came, we were getting wet, were getting sick. But all that didn't matter because-- and we stayed in the ghetto, cooking. We were cooking. There was a field right next to the-- to this-- next to the barracks. And again, one day they would have us moving the brick from one side the other. And the next, they're putting him back where originally. This is about all. There already, from our own community-- there were people from other community. Neighboring communities were also in that. Had nervous breakdowns, and they were in horrible condition. And the Nazis would come in and beat them, get on top of them. They had to be tied down. And it was horrible sights to see for children. Surrounded with Nazis, you couldn't escape. You couldn't come in. You couldn't get out. And this is how we lived for about five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard for me to estimate. But it was a big camp, was a very big, big camp. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for-- did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us some food, yes. I cannot even remember whether it was kosher because I know my mother would-- whether they would give us some dry produce that you should cook yourself. But in the field, they would have a little fire. And on top of that little fire, they would cook something. It was-- it was very rough. I know there was hunger. Once our neighbors, they allowed that if you had some connections from the outside, they allowed you to bring in the food. So once our neighbor was very nice and they sent us a ham. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't eat it. My mother says well, we're alive. While there is substitution, we're never going to touch it. My father, to him, the feeding was-- didn't much because he didn't have hope. He sort of started losing hope. But yet he was moving. And there was always-- they had big rabbis, all kinds. And they all lived with hope that this is temporary, and this is-- we're going to go home. We're going to live a normal life and all that. Then, they started. The camp was liquidated, I think, in four sessions. But after-- I mean, we were in the third part of the group. Like every week would be a train going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that's where it was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We didn't know where it was going. They didn't tell us anything. But they said they were going  to take us in a place where we're all going to be together, and there was going to be housing, like everything. In the meantime, oh yes, they were also in the ghettos again. Any valuable possessions, any money, anything that you have, give it up because you're going to be severely punished. Home, it was from the Hungarians. In Berehovo, it was from the Nazis.
